CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Catholic Truth Conference of Ireland begins on
Wednesday evening next, October 10th, with an address
in the Round Room, Rotunda, by his Lordship the Most
Rev. Dr. Clancy, Bishop of Elphin, on “Secularism in
Education.”” On the following day three papers,
one on “The Want of a Catholic Book Review,”
by Mr. Francis Swesney, and another on the very in-
teresting subject,  The Catholic Church and Socialism,’”
by Father James M'Caffrey, S.T.L., Maynooth College,
will ba read at the sitting from 11 a.m. to 1.30 p.m., and
at the afternoon at 3 p.m. Mr. J. J. Horgan, of Cork,
will read a paper on ‘“ Lay Co-operation in the Work of
the Society.’”” On Thursday evening the second annual
reception will take place at the Gresham Hotel. The
first reception last year was an unqualified success, and

s

it was a function that was important as well as enjoy-
able. Social gatherings of Catholics as Catholics from
various parts of the country are useful and desirable
for many reasons. There are so many movements that
are, and rightly so, what is termed “ non-sectarianm,”
that there is a real danger of Irish Catholic public
opinion becoming somewhat stifled. The more we have
““non-sectarian ’’ bodies in Ireland the more we need
professedly Catholic bodies by way of preserving the
balance. 5

There is one suggestion we would make to the authori-
ties of the Catholic Truth Society in connection with
this Conference. The membership of the Society is, in
comparison with what it reasonably ought to be, miser-
ably small. If we can trust our memory we think it
was somewhere in the region of 600 at the time of the
last Conference. It ought at least be 6,000. Probably
the authorities hesitate to be what they might consider
too pushful in recruiting members during the Confer-
ence. However, in view of the comparatively small
membership perhaps they might see their way to make
a special effort during this Conference. We would
suggest that arrangements be made at cach meeting,
not excluding the reception, for the taking of names and
annual subscriptions from new members, and that a$
each meeting the Chairman should request as many of
the non-members present as might see their way to de
50 to join the Society before leaving the meeting. We
are satisfied ourselves that hundreds who attend the
Conference would join, that hundreds actually intend te
join, but as they aro not taken on the hop, if we may
use the expression, they don’t join, and the Society,
which is doing admirable work, is the loser.

We have before us the Parliamentary Return issued
at the request of Mr. John Dillon, showing the amount
paid by the Department to each officer of the I.A.O.S.
whose services were utilised by the Department for giv-
ing instruction in agricultural subjects and organising
agricultural, home industries and agricultural credit
societies. The Return covers the period from 1st April,
1900, to the 28th February, 1905. During the year
ending 31st March, 1901, our friend, now happily gone
back to the land from whence he came, Viggo Schwartz,
got £100 in salary and £223 7s. 11d. by way of expenses
from the Department. On January lst, 1903, Viggo
got an increase, and his salary became £130. We see
that according to the Parliamentary Return that the
“gsober Nationalist,”” Mr. George Russell, the bard of
spooks, whose rate of salary was £250, received
£67 9s. 10d. of that salary from the Department during
the eleven months ending 28th February, 1905, and the
expenses he drew outi of the public purse during the same
period amounted to £114 0Os. 1d.. Travelling at the
expense of the funds of the Department was evidently &
“soft thing’’ for this shrewd and worldly wise poet.
Amongst other payments by the Department to the
I.A.0.S., we see item 1900-01, recoupment of fees in
respect of lectures delivered by Messrs. Russell and Han-
non, £59 17s.; item 1901-02, do., do., £255 3s.; item
1902-03, do., do., £167 12s. The amounts paid in
travelling and other expenses by the Department to the
I.A.0.8. officials are very heavy. For instance, during
the year ending March 31st, 1902, they amounted to

£2,179 1bs. 5d., and during the following year fo *

£3,027 12s. 9d. Altogether, during the four years and

-eleven months covered by the Report, a sum of

£10,988 6s. 1d. was paid away in expenses alone! The
total payments came to £16,916 18s. 8d. ;

It will interest our readers to point out that a niece
of Mr. Michael Sullivan, Miss Nora Sullivan, of Mil-
town-Malbay, took first place in all Ireland in the Senior
Grade Intermediate Examinations this year. Mr. Sulli-
van has done great work in the matter of opening up
railway company clerkships to competitive examination,
and it is particularly appropriate that a niece of his
should show what an Irish Catholic girl can do in face
of the competitions from all quarters at the Interme-
diate. Miss Nora Sullivan, besides an exhibition of £40
and a prize of £10, won three book prizes of £4 each,
and two medals.

In the course of a letter a Queenstown correspondent
writes:—“I was much pleased to see the reference to
‘the staffing of the Belfast Technical Schools. For
months have I waited in the vain hope that someone
better qualified than I am would present to the public in
all its nakedness the extraordinary state of things that
exists in this southern township. Last November, in
the interests of justice and fair play, I sent to the
Cork Ezaminer a mild form of protest against the action
of the Cork Technical Committee re the appointment
of Principal to the Queenstown Day Preparatory and
Technical School. My article never appeared though
I gave my name in proof of my bona fides. While not
wishing to encroach too much on your valuable space,
yet to make myself clear I must ask you to allow me
space for a shont account of the establishment of Tech-
nical Education here. Some time towards the end of
1902, at the request of the local Technical Committee,
the Presentation Brothers kindly gave the use of their
laboratory—their manual instruction room and a class-
room for evening classes. Instructors were appointed,
and work commenced the first week in January, 1903.
The classes continued to be held in the college during
the years 1903 and 1904 to the end of the summer ses-
sion, 1905. In 1905 negotiations were started with the
object of procuring the lately-vacated Young Men’s
Society rooms and converting them into a Technical
Institute.  The negotiations were successful, and a
grant-in-aid was obtained. Now comes the interesting
part of this article. ~ Of course, the school should have
a head-master, and naturally such a position should
‘be largely advertised in order to give the best men a
chance of competing. But no—the position was never
advertised, but George Thompson, Esq., Teacher
of Building Construction and Practical Woor-
work, a ‘Saved’ Englishman, was appointed principal.
‘Would it not have been more decent to have advertised
with the addendum, ‘No Irishman or Catholic necd
apply ¥

Our correspondent, goes on :—“ I do not question M.
Thompson’s ability, but I do say, without fear of con-
tradiction, that men equally, if not better, qualified,
.and Irishmen and Catholics to boot, would have applied
for the vacancy were they but permitted. Of the other
professors I content myself by saying that the ‘ Saved ’
element largely preponderates. I enclose you a marked
copy of prospectus presumably drawn up by the Prin-
cipal, and I would direct your special attention to page
32, where the English and Irish languages are referred
to. I daresay the much-maligned printer’s devil is
.saddled with the vagaries in orthography, or pevhaps
the writer has taken a leaf from the Fzaminer’s book.
But what about the rules we heard so much of in our
school-boy days regarding concord and government in
English compositions? Now the people of Queenstown,
in season and out of season, are ever ready to ‘ resqluce.’
We have passed with acclamation resolutions without

estion. Of course, we condemn the Queen’s Colleges
‘and Trinity in the most scathing terms, but cannot we
‘be .cansistent? Why condemn them? Why refuse to
end our sons there on the plea of religion when we have

where the very same religious disabilities obbain?

ears old and upwards?’

The prospectus of the “ Queenstown '’ Day Prepara-

them to express in proper English that which %e desires

tion, idioms, and other necessary elements.”” The
italics are ours. The ¢ construction of sentences’’

Under the heading. “Irish’’ we read:—* Owing to
the rapid development of the movement for the rescusi-

commercial purposes that the future business man
should possess a knowledge of Irish in its commercial
aspect. It has therefore been decided to introduce this
subject into the school Cirriculum.’” The italics are,
but the spelling is not ours. By the way, what pre-
cisely is a “knowledge of Irish in its commercial
aspect ¥’

their children, but Patrick, though not congenial as a
name, is laid under contribution when sectarian capital
may be made out of it. Why, is it not even said that
St. Patrick was a Protestant?

There is a debt of £5,400 on this school.

The chief work: before'the Gaelic League'is to secure
Ivish as a living language. What is a living language!
thd].\'er Dinneen, in the course of his lecture in Belfast,
said : —

In modern times and under the stress of modern civilisation a

living language can survive only in so far as it is able to hold
its own in literary, social, and commercial life. To deserve to
be called a living language it must prevail as the vernacular
over a continuous area of considerable extent. It must be the
natural and sufficient medium in which men of education and
literary ability can express themselv It must be the medium
of social intercourse within that area. It must bo capable of
adapting itsclf to the requirements of modern commerce. It
1ust be the ordinary medium .of education,

It is easy, because it is pleasant, to fall into a state
of false security concerning the Irish language revival.
Too much “movement ’’ and too little language is suffi-
cient to soothe many Irish-Ireland enthusiasts; fortu-
nately Father Dinneen has a very keen perception of
the realities of the situation, and fortunately also he is
fond of intruding his clear views on an Irish-Ireland
world that, being human, is wishful to be in good con-
ccit of itself. Father Dinneen said:—

The organisation of the Irish districts is a matter that admits
of no delay if the language is to be saved. Meetings should be
held, and a public spirit favourable to the general use of Irish
within these areas aroused and fostered. Attention should be
drawn at these meetings to the influence of shops and all such
institutions in discouraging the use of Irish. An organised
effort should be made to prevent the language getting into disuse
in public in such places as fairs, markets, and the like. An
effort should be made to prevent the waste of emergy that is
going on at present on the part of the people in endeavouring
to speak bad and broken English when their work could be done
just as well by speaking good Irish.

There are some people who have'so much exaltation,
and so much sentiment, that they can’t see straight.
Father Dinneen’s numerous published works, to go no
further, are evidence:of the reality of his passion for
the realisation of an Irish Ireland, and his views, as
often expressed on the Irish districts question, are a
standing testimony to his level head. He said:—

It is impossible to solve the problem of the Irish districts
withoup taking fully into account the peculiar circumstances of
these districts. We must bear in mind the poverty of the
districts, the difficulty of providing for youug men and women
after they have left school in any other way than the desperate
remedy of emigration, the necessity of providing for these
districts industries congenial to the soil, and of working these
industries mainly through the medium of the Irish language.

Further on Father Dinneen declared :— !

The Gaelic League has now got well into its teens. It has got
into a stereotyped method of procceding. It is, I believe, spread-
ing. But while it spreads the vernacular areas are shrinking,
and the quality of the language in these arcas is yearly
deteriorating. Is the Gaelic League to go on indefinitely in this
g!el'eotypcd groove, as the language tide subsides to the vanish-
inz point? Will a decade or two deoades of years realise one's
Wworst fears, which may he expressed by an adaptation of the
words of the song: “ The League was still there but the Language
was gone!” Addressing, as I am, such an important meeting
of sympathisers with the:Language movement, convened under
the auspices of such an influential’ body® as the Belfast Coiste
Ceanntair, T cannot refrain from expressing my scttled. conviction

: /
that the Gaelic League and its Executive body, if they merely

“ continue to work in the groove in which they have been moving’

for the last couple of yoars, will never succeed ‘in arresting tho:
decay that is at this moment striking at the heart of ourl
national specch (hear, hear).  The occasion calls for a new
departure, for moroe concentrated effort, for special attention to
the Irish-speaking districts. I appeal to the Executive body of
the Gaelic League, a body some fifty strong, of which somo
prominent members are present in this hall, to give a lead:im
this matter. It would, of course, be a mistake to expect too
much from this body of fifty, a large propertion of whom have
no natural sympathy with the inhabitants of the Irish districts.
But it is quite within their power to map out these <_hsmczs, to
put the vast body of Gaelic Leaguers in possession of the
geographical and cconomical facts that bear on the problem, to
inaugurate and carry out a series of meetings in those districts
in which the spirit of the people may be roused and practical
remedies for the undue encroachment of English and for the
strengthening of the native language preached to the people
from a hundred platforms (applause). Speaking last year at
Dungarvan, T gave a similar advice. I regret to say it has not
been acted much on. I now repeat it. If our Exccutive hody of:
fifty, cleoted with such a flourish of trumpets by three hundred
representatives of the League branches, are unable or unwilling
to undertake this work, then as the years roll on, their annual
clection will become one of the farces with which our history is
interspersed.

Father Dinneen has boldly appealed to the people;
and we trust that the people will give adequate con-
sideration to his warnings and views.

The arrangement of classes for the Leinster College of
Irish has now been made in outline. The classes will
mest every Monday and Thursday—beginners from 7
to 8 p.m.; lectures on phonetics from 8§ to 9 p.m.;
advanced classes from 9 to 10 p.m. also under the charge
of the professors. The direct method will be followed;
but the method will not be a purely oral one, while the:
lectures on phonetics will be devoted to practice rather
than to theory. Students who intend to teach Irish
will be selected to teach criticism ** lessons under the
supervision and advice of the professors. It has been
found necessary to postpone the opening of the classes
till the middle of the present month. The premises of
the College at 52 Upper O’Connell St., Dublin, will be
ready for occupation immediately. Intending students
may obtain information by writing the Registrar, Mr.
Eamonn Ceannt, at that address, or calling any evening
from 5 to 6, or from 8 to 9; Saturdays 7 to 9. Sub-
scriptions to the College are coming in, and include one
of £10 from the Very Rev. Dr. Delaney, S.J., President
University College. Intending subscribers may address:
the Hon. Treasurer, Rev. M. Ronan, Archbishop’s
House, Dublin.

The annual report of St. Angela’s College and High
School, Cork, presents a successful record. We read
in the report “that all the students, 9 1n numbe_r, who
went in for examination were successf}xl, two. _takmg the
Degree Mental and Moral Science, with Latin; 4 pass-
ing 1st Arts Course and 3 the 2nd. In addition to these
tho Matriculation Class had 4 students successful. Had
the distribution of prizes been fixed for a moth later
we should hope to have added 6 more to the list, as 3
of the Senior Grade pupils of this year went in for
Matriculation on the 18th, and 3 of the Honour Uni-

of October.””

At the Intermediate examinations an exhibition, two
prizes and other distinctions were won in the Middle
Grade and one prize and twenty-four honours in the
Junior Grade, high places being secured in Irish in the
Middle, Junior, and Preparatory Grades. The 5

ment, and we learn that there are five classes in Physics .

There will be a big night at the Father Mathew Hall
on Monday next to honour the anniversary of the birth
of the great Apostle of Temperance. Mr. P. J. O'Neill,
Chairman of the Dublin County Council, is announced
to deliver the anniversary oration, and those who know
Mr. O’Neill’s ability as a public speaker will not need
to be told to look forward to an interesting address.
Rev. T. A. Finlay, Rev. Fr. Paul, Mr. R. Hazelton,
M.P., are also announced to speak.

There was a ‘“ grand ’’ concert recently at Rosario

School, Ballynafeigh, “Belfast. We hear that Ballyna-
feigh is a sort of Wrauthmines in Belfast, and so, -per-
haps, it is not to be wondered at that there was not a
song in Irish on the programme. But if there was no
Irish song on the programme there were plenty of

THE RATHMINES AUDIT, MR. SULLIVAN
AND MR. KING.

R. MICHAEL SULLIVAN is not satisfied with

the rulings of Mr. King, the Local Government

Board auditor, on some of the objections which he

lodged at the recent Rathmines audit. He is not satis-

fied with Mr. King’s subsequent reasons for his. rulings,

and so Mr. Sullivan has appealed to the Local Govern-
ment Board upon the matter. -

We do not see how Mr. Sullivan, or anyone else,
could be expected to be satisfied with Mr. King’s
rulings; no doubt in this country holders of highly-paid
patronage jobs like the Local Government Board
auditorships think that the people ought to be satisfied
with anything, if indeed they do not hold that it is
impertinence on the part of the people to ask for any
service from the highly-paid officers of Dublin Castle
Government.

Mr. King, in his 1905 report on Rathmines affairs,
remarked that he challenged the legality of a payment
of £800 for a site for a proposed public library, but as
the Council had taken steps (by way of a Bill in Parlia-
ment) to legalise the payment he allowed the matter to
stand. It is important to note that the objection to the
legality of this payment was first raised by Mr. King
himself. In fact it was in consequence of Mr. King
pressing this important objection to this payment of
£800 that the Council’s solicitor decided to put in a
clause in the Bill of 1905 setting the matter right. But
the Bill of 1905 did not pass; and the Bill of 1906, that
contained the clause also, was ignominiously kicked out:
of the House of Commons. As the Bill containing the
clause has not become law Mr. King’s original objection
stands in its full force. But Mr. King is apparently
a bit of an acrobat as well as an auditor. The payment
to which he himself was the first to object has now,
according to himself, become legal! He answers Mr.
Sullivan’s objection by stating—‘‘ That the payment for
the Library site out of Loans Fund Account was not
illegal.”” In 1905 he challenged its legality, but let it
stand in hopes of the Rathmines Bill passing; in 1906,
the Bill not having passed, he simply turns an auditorial «
somersault and declares that what he challenged the
previous year has since become quite legal! Now the
Council’s powers are limited by the Libraries Act to a
rate of 1d.in the £, and the payment of £800 for a site,
in addition to the expenditure on the existing Library,
brought the total beyond the authorised legal limit.
But this plainly illegal expenditure, the objection to
which originated with Mr. King himself, is now declared
by Mr. King to be legal! No wonder Mr. Sullivan i
not satisfied and has appealed to the Local Government
Board. : . : z

legal expenses incurred by the Council as a consequenc
of the Local Government Board inquiry into the Wards

rather do we find the explanation in the general state-

sion of self-government, have had the way clear to fight
for principles of Democracy, Ireland, being subject to
an alien rule, has had her energies largely monopolized
in fighting for principles of Nationality.

Unless a people governed by a foreign Power agree to
pay submissive recognition to that Power it invariably
happens that the political activity of the subject people
is mainly directed towards attaining to freedom from
that authority which they refuse to accept. True, this
ultimate goal may occasionally seem to be overshadowed
by certain political questions of large importance; but
obscured though it may become now and then, the
vision of national freedom is never absolutely obliter-
ated. If subordinate problems should at times deflect
the people’s encrgies from the main object, we find that
the greater struggle is presently resumed with increased
vigour.  Self-government being the ideal, its pursuit
places outside the barrier of public agitation those finer
issues of a politico-philosophic character; and thus,
questions as to the form of government, or as to aristo-
cratic privilege and the like, occupy no place in the im-
mediate hopes and desires of the populace. It has been
so in Ireland.

With regard to the great warfare waged in modern
times by Democracy against Arvistocracy, Ireland has
stood a more or less silent spectator. ‘The Rights of
Man ’’ has never been a rallying cry amongst us; and
whilst we may have benefited by the forceful sweep of
democratic progress which the last century has wit-
nessed, we, certainly, have never immersed ourselves in
the combat of which that progress was born. In order
the hetter to realize our position in respect of the fiercely
militant antagonism that has divided the two classes of
the community, let us glance at the situation in Ireland
at certain periods when this great class-warfare in other
countries raged the loudest.

In 1832 the clamour of the populace in England for
Parliamentary Reform reached its point of culmination.
Notwithstanding the violent character of the agitation
in the country that admirable institution of rather :.oLle
noblemen, the House of Lords, persisted in blocking
the way. And it was not until the King threatened to
create new peers in order to get Lord John Russell’s
great Reform Bill carried through the Upper Chamber
that their lordships agreed to that Bill. Its passage
into law marked an epoch in the advancement of
Democracy as the Bill destroyed aristocratic influence
in the Boroughs. Now in Ireland at that time (1832)
the people knew little and cared less about democratic

- principles. Dragged as they were after the tail of a
foreign Power for which they had no consuming affec-
tion, our fathers based their political agitation not on
the demand for ‘ popular’’ rights, but on that for
National rights. Repeal of the Union had begun to
grip the Irish mind, and several Repeal Associations
had already heen suppressed by the Government. In
the general election of 1832 forty Repealers were elected
to Parliament, and two years subsequent O’Connell

" formally introduced the question of Repeal in the House

of Commons.

Consider again sixteen years after. The year 1848
was the great revolutionary period in Europe. At no
‘other time is there exposed to us more clearly the dif-
ferent nature of the Irish struggle as compared with
the contemporary political strife of other European
countries. On the Continent the “ people’s rights’’
were being asserted in the language of war. In France,
Germany, Italy and Austria Democracy was in open
conflict with the opposing class, and it seemed that all
other political questions had been swept aside by the
cyclopean force concentrated in the arena where Demo-
cracy and Aristocracy clutched at each other’s throats.
It was essentially the period of a fighting and a conquer-
ing Demccracy. Turning now to Ireland we find there,

Thus, as in 1832 and 1848, the cry was not “ Advance

From this rapid glance we see that during the last

tion of that great question, however, it must be admitted
that they saw clearly enough what Ireland wantgd, and,
considering the times and the Ireland in which they
lived, I think it must be granted that they planned
! wisely and well. And they fought for what they wanted
wisely and well enough, too.  Most of the demands
they made have since been conceded, and if we to-day
have a miore or less clear field for the pressing of our
. claims for a proper Irish educational system, not a little
of the credit is due to those who cleared the decks for
1 One matter is to be regretted. In neglecting the

‘educational question they left the country to leak its
" nationality, with the result that the children of the

parents they partially emancipated are, in a great
measure, careless whether they finish the act of eman-
cipation or not. Out of the comparatively free and
might-be prosperous Ireland of to-day, her sons are run-
ning with as great and with far more shameless haste
than they did out of the landlord-ridden and hopeless
Treland of thirty years ago. :

To conclude, Ireland thirty years ago was, as it were,
in the coils of the sexpent—Landlordism. In her despe-
rate position, however, clear she, in the presence of her
leaders, could see what she wanted to restore her to
liberty and nationhood, she, for the moment, could do
nothing beyond tearing with feet and hands at, and
writhing to break the grip of, the enemy at her throat.

pillar-laying butterflies of the British Press—were creep-

g or fluttering at their own sweet w1.11 about and over

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDOWMENT—XXXVIIIL

“Illegal Foundation And Maintenance of The Ennis
Grammar School, of All The English Schools, of Bruns-
wick Street School, of Harcourt Street School.”

’I‘ HE Act of 1723 gave the Governors of the

Erasmus Smith Schools authority to found “ one
or more English School or Schools * throughout Ireland
out of an alleged ““surplus’’ which it defined. As
there has been no “surplus’’ as defined by the Act of
1723, there has been no money to be expended on the
foundation or maintenance of any of the above Schools,
and the £300,000 that have been expended on those

Schools have been illegally expended, and con-
stitute a most gross violation of the Trusts. But the
law-breaking and trust-breaking do not stop here.

There has been a series of illegal acts in connection
with the foundation, the maintenance, and the working
of these Schools.

The Governors founded the Ennis Grammar School
in 1773. On the building and repairs of it, and on the
Master’s residence, they have expended £4,284. The
School has been discontinued. X

The Act of 1723 gave legal authority to found Eng-
lish Schools out of an alleged surplus.’”” It gave no
authority to found a Grammar School; and the Charter
had forbidden to found more than three Grammar
Schools.  So that the foundation and maintenance of
the Ennis Grammar School were on this ground also
illegal. They kept free boys in this School. This was
also illegal.  The Charter has commanded that free
boys should be kept only in the three Free Schools. And
the Governors had interpolated the 2nd of Erasmus
Smith’s Rules, had added the words “or shall be
erected '’ in order to give a show of reason for keeping
free boys in Blue Coat Hospital, in this School, and in
their other Schools.

The Governors built the first English School in Valen-
cia in 1790. They have built 146 of those Schools
throughout Ireland. They had no legal authority to
build even one. For they got legal authority to build
English Schools only out of a “surplus’’ which has
never existed. But the law-breaking went further.

(@) They gave free education to twenty children liv-
ing within two miles of each of those Schools—on the
strength of the forged Rule. ;

(%) They took out two classes of illegal leases of the
sites or plots on which the Schools have been built.
By the first class of leasé they bound themselves to
hand over the plot and building to the owner of the
soil whenever the School should be discontinued. By
the second class of lease they enabled the owner of the
soil to close the School, and take over the plot and
building whenever the Bible was not read, and. the:
Protestant Catechism was not taught in the School
(Rep. 1857, Vol. II., pp. 317-321).

Both these classes of leases were illegal. The Act of
1723 gave the Governors authority to build English
Schools out of an alleged “surplus;’’ it gave them no
authority to give away any part of the property of the
Endowment. At least £60,000 have been illegally
given away in this manner. The Governors have ex-
pended £39,475 on building eighty-two English Schools
(Rep. 1857, Vol. II., p. 124). They have expended
£70{000, or £75,000, on building 146 English Schools.
‘Only forty-three of those buildings were used as Schools

Bt

in 1891. Now there are not probably more than twenty
used as Schools. So that, under those illegal leases,
ab least £60,000 of the Endowment have illegally beon
given away to strangers. The Governors, for instance,
expended £1,411 on building an English School in the
Coombe, Dublin. The School was discontinued in 1847,
and the building was handed over to Lord Meath. They
expended £2,500 on building a Schoolhouse in St.
Mark’s Parish, Dublin. I do not know is the Schoo!
still in existence.

The annual expense of the English Schools was, in

1870, £4,500. The total expenditure on them cannot
be much short—if anything—of £300,000, all of which
has beon paid away without any legal authority what-
ever, and in the most gross violation of the Trusts.
. The Governors founded the Brunswick Street School
in 1811 (Rep. 1879, pp. 99-100). They have rented a
plot of land at tho back of the Antient Concert Rooms
at £50 a year. There are four temants on this land
that pay them £48 1s. 6d. a year. They have called
this the “ Dublin Property,”” which happens to be a
minus quantity of £1 1s. 6d. a year. On this property
they have established a Grammar School, and have
be:n educating fifteen free boys in it—all in violation
of law and Trusts, as the following prove:—

(@) There is no ‘“surplus’’ from which they could
found any School.

(b)) They have no legal authority to found a Grammar
School even if they had a surplus.

(c) They have no legal authority to keep free boys
except in the Grammar Schools of Drogheda, Galway

and Tipperary, the forgery notwithstanding.

The Governors appear to have surpassed themselves
in law-breaking in the foundation and maintenance of
the Harcourt Street Grammar School, which they have
called “ The High School.”” They founded this School
about the year 1870. They purchased the rented house
and land on which it stands, in 1868, for £4,000. They
have expended £4,260 more on buildings and equip-
ment. To meet this expense they sold out £5,000
stock, and paid the remainder, £3,260, out of income
(Rep. 1879, pp. 96-118-119, and draft scheme of Gover-
nors). They have educated twenty free boys every
year, sometimes more, in this School. They have given
to its pupils Exhibitions of £40 a year in Trinity Col-
lege. In doing all this they have violated the law and
the Trust in the following respects:—

(a) In selling out stock. They have no more autho-
rity to sell stock than they have to sell land.  The
Charter has specially forbidden them to sell land. And
the Chancery Law would allow them to sell land or
stock only when such sale would be advantageous to
the charity. The sale was injurious to the charity.
For it not only took away so much money from it, but
established an institution in Harcourt Street that
drained away from it some £1,500 a year for many
years.

(5) In buying Harcourt Street School. For they have
got from tho Charter permission to buy property only
for the support of the three Free Grammar Schools at
Drogheda, Galway and Tipperary, and for the other
Trusts among which is not the education of Dublin
children.  For the Charter (p. 195) says:— And fur-
ther, know ye, that we . do give and grant,
unto the said Governors and their successors, for ever

our power and authority to get purchase
% for the maintenance, sustentation and relief
of all and every person and persons to be placed in the
said, Schools, lands, tenements and heredita-
ments.”” It then says of all the lands, tenements, etc.,
which the Trustees shall acquire, that they “shall be

wholly employed in, and applied unto, the sustentation
and maintenance of the several charitable uses herein

pressly command that whatever lands, etc., the Gover-
nors should acquire by purchase shoul.d be wholly em-
loyed in the maintenance, sustentation a,.ud relief of
all ‘scholars placed in the said Schools, and in the main-
tenance of tho several other charitable uses expressed
in tho Charter. They, therefore, forbid that lands or
tenements should be purchased in Dublin for the foun-
dation of a School there for the education of Dublin
children. Even if the Governors had purchased land
or tenements, and that purchase were for the advance-
ment of the charity, if thero were tenants’ children on
the purchased land, they could give them free educa-
tion only in the Grammar Schools of Drogheda, Galway
and Tipperary. 3 3 3

(c) In giving free education to twenty Dublin chil-
dren in the Harcourt Street School_. It is only the
poor children living within two miles of the Free
Schools in Drogheda, Galway and Tipperary who are
entitled to free education. The forgery will not do.
The Governors have just as much right to found an
Erasmus Smith Grammar School on Spion Kop, and
educato twenty blacks free in it as they have had to
establish Harcourt Street School. They have only to
purchase a house on Spion Kop, equip it as a School,
and pay the Masters, and the thing is done.

(@) In founding a Grammar School in Harcourt
Strect.

(¢) In giving the pupils of this School Exhibitions
in Trinity College. The pupils of Lord Kitchener’s
School in Khartoum have just as much right to Eras-
mus Smith Exhibitions.

(f) In paying for all those things out of a “surplus’’ }
that never existed. i

The defence of the foundation and maintenance of {
the Harcourt Street School, which has been made by !
Lord Justice Fitzgibbon and ex-Vice-Chancellor Chat- |
terton, has displayed a wonderful elasticity of law, c}
finance, and statement. The ex-Vice-Chancellor (Rep.
1879, p. 96) describes and defends the genesis of the
Harcourt Street School. He says that the Statute of
1723 authorised the Governors to found English Schools
out of the surplus, and that the Statute of 1833 had ;
made the Treasurer’s Poundage—6d. in the £ of the
gross income—a free fund for them. He says that
they founded an English School out of the surplus, and
endowed in that School a classical department out of
the Treasurer’s Poundage. He contends that they had
thus legally founded the Grammar School in Harcourt
Street, although they had no legal authority to found a
fourth Grammar School. And Lord Justice Fitzgibbon
has said (Rep. 1894, p. 3) that the foundation was legal,
and that the legal difficulty was got over by applying
the Treasurer’s Poundage to the establishment of that
School.

At the Public Sitting in 1892 (Rep. 1894, pp. 31-2)
Father Humphreys told Lord Justice Fitzgibbon and
Vice-Chancellor Chatterton that, in getting out of the
legal difficulty, they had got into a financial difficulty
out of which they could not escape. He pointed oub -
to them that the Board had expended more than £8,000
on buying, building and equipping Harcourt Street
School ; that this sum at five per cent. would realise
£400 a year; that the Vice-Chancellor had stated thab
they had to pay out of their income to the support of
Harcourt Street School more than £800 a year—thus
making the whole expense more than £1,200 a year, .
and that. the cost of an English School was at most
£100 or £200 a year. t 3

CUCHULAIN AND OIBIN.

The stories of Cuchulain and Oisin_particularly lend
themselves to dramatic treatment. In these days of
Irish Ireland they would, no doubt, find most appro-
priate expression in the languago of tho Ireland of their
day. Yot the great number of people who unfortunately
have to depend on English for their acquaintance with
Irish heroic themes makes a little volume just published
very acceptable. Cuchulain, a cyclo of Irish plays by
Suseen Varian, with “Oisin the Hero,” by John Varian,
is that referred to. It will be recollected that Miss
Varian is the author of a powerful temperance dramatic
sketch, which was produced with much success at the All-
Ireland Temperance Bazaar at Ballsbridge. The present
interesting cycle of plays from her pen begin with the
wooing of Emer, by Cuchulain, and end with his death.
“Qisin the Hero,” written by Mr, John Varian, deals
with the return of the poet of the Fianna from the land
of the young.  One often hears the peasant in Mr.
Yeats’ drama, who responds to the call of Cathleen ni
Houlihan, quoted as the personification of Irish fidelity
to national ideals. In Oisin’s answer to Niamh, spurn-
ing a life of indulgence to return to Iveland, we meet -
a beautiful oxpression of the same truth—* But Oisin
raised his voico in indignation and sorrow: ‘I will not
sleep your sleep of forgetfulness, beautiful lady, for I
would rather have one hour of sorrow for my heroes of
Erinn than a life-time here in your land of delight, for-
gebting their glory.”’’  Messrs. Sealy, Bryers and
Walker have printed and published the book, and. it
should be mentioned that they have turned it out
excellently. 0.
